text: A polarized training intensity distribution (~80% low intensity below ventilatory threshold, ~12% moderate, ~8% high intensity) produces significantly greater performance improvements than a threshold-heavy distribution (~67% low, ~25% moderate, ~8% high) over a 5-month training period.
Runners following a polarized training distribution improved 10.4 km cross-country race time by 157 seconds compared to 121.5 seconds for those following a threshold-heavy distribution (p = 0.03).

Reducing moderate-intensity training volume (between ventilatory and respiratory compensation thresholds) in favor of more low-intensity training improves endurance performance, even when high-intensity training volume is held constant.
High-intensity training above the respiratory compensation threshold should comprise approximately 8% of total training volume to be sufficient for performance gains in a polarized model.

Spending excessive time in the moderate-intensity zone (between ventilatory threshold and respiratory compensation threshold) may compromise endurance adaptations compared to keeping the majority of training below the ventilatory threshold.
Training intensity distribution should be controlled using heart rate zones anchored to individually determined ventilatory and respiratory compensation thresholds from maximal exercise testing.
